source.zh: 《墨西哥人绘画史》（约1530年代）；《诸太阳传说》（《奇马尔波波卡手抄本》，1558年，纳瓦特尔语）；贝尔纳迪诺·德·萨阿贡《佛罗伦萨手抄本》第七卷；《墨西哥志》（Histoyre du Mechique，安德烈·泰韦的法译本，底本为一份已佚的西班牙文稿）；《梵蒂冈手抄本A》（里奥斯抄本）；太阳石（墨西哥国立人类学博物馆藏）
source.en: Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas (1530s); Leyenda de los Soles (Codex Chimalpopoca, 1558, in Nahuatl); Bernardino de Sahagún, Florentine Codex, Book 7; Histoyre du Mechique, André Thevet's French version of a lost Spanish manuscript; Codex Vaticanus A (Codex Ríos); the Sun Stone, National Museum of Anthropology, Mexico City

note.zh:
讲创世的阿兹特克材料，一份都不是征服之前写的。前殖民时期留下来的是图像抄本（博尔吉亚组那类），它们是占卜书和历书，不叙事；把故事讲成文字的，全是1530—1570年代用拉丁字母记的：《墨西哥人绘画史》（一般追到方济各会士奥尔莫斯的圈子）、纳瓦特尔语的《诸太阳传说》（1558）、萨阿贡《佛罗伦萨手抄本》第七卷、以及泰韦法译的《墨西哥志》（底本已佚，通常也追到奥尔莫斯）。执笔和提问的是传教士，问法本身会塑造答案——洪水一节与《创世记》像到什么程度是原住民自己的说法、什么程度是被问出来的，分不干净。

各家版本互相打架，尤其在太阳纪上。《诸太阳传说》的次序是虎、风、雨、水，年数676、364、312、676；《墨西哥人绘画史》次序大体相同，但给每一纪指派一位神去当那个太阳——依次是特斯卡特利波卡、克察尔科亚特尔、特拉洛克、查尔奇乌特利奎——后两位并不在双神所生的四个儿子里；《梵蒂冈手抄本A》从水纪开头，年数一路上千。太阳石中央「四运动」四周的方框，通常读作虎、风、雨、水，但这块石头整体该怎么解释，学界至今没有定论。本文按《诸太阳传说》排。

这条时间线是拼的，古人未必把它当一个连贯故事讲。十三重天与四兄弟出自《墨西哥人绘画史》；撕裂大地兽只见于《墨西哥志》，纳瓦特尔语文献里没有；下米克特兰取骨与粮山盗玉米出自《诸太阳传说》；特奥蒂瓦坎点火出自萨阿贡。不同城邦讲法本就不同，本文取的是墨西哥谷的说法。造地这一段接得最生硬：《墨西哥人绘画史》里，大地是直接拿水中那头巨兽西帕克特利的身体做的；《墨西哥志》里，被撕成两半的是特拉尔特库特利。原文是两套说法，后人常把两者并成同一头「大地兽」，本文把它们前后接起来，是编辑上的取舍。特斯卡特利波卡缺一只脚，是图像上的定式（断处画成一面冒烟的黑曜石镜或一条蛇），但16世纪的文字材料没交代这只脚是怎么丢的；流传很广的「被大地兽咬掉」出自后世复述，正文不写。另外两点：把「奥梅特奥特尔」当成一位至高的二元之神，主要是20世纪学者（尤其莱昂-波尔蒂利亚）的整合，这个名字在16世纪文献里几乎见不到，原始记载给的是一对神和一个地名奥梅约坎，哈利（Richard Haly）等人质疑过「阿兹特克有一位唯一至高神」这种读法；特拉尔特库特利的名字意思是「大地之主」，语法上是阳性，造像却多作女性，本文按《墨西哥志》的用法称她。
note.en:
Not one Aztec account of creation was written down before the conquest. What survives from before it are pictorial books of the Borgia type, which are divinatory almanacs and do not narrate. Every narrative version was set down in the Latin alphabet between the 1530s and the 1570s: the Historia de los mexicanos por sus pinturas (usually traced to the circle of the Franciscan Andrés de Olmos), the Nahuatl Leyenda de los Soles of 1558, Book 7 of Sahagún's Florentine Codex, and the Histoyre du Mechique, Thevet's French rendering of a lost Spanish text also generally traced to Olmos. Friars asked the questions and held the pen, and the questions shape the answers; how much of the flood episode's resemblance to Genesis is native and how much is an artefact of the asking cannot be cleanly separated.

The versions contradict each other, most visibly over the world-ages. The Leyenda de los Soles runs jaguar, wind, rain, water, at 676, 364, 312 and 676 years. The Historia de los mexicanos keeps roughly that order but names a god to serve as the sun in each age, in turn Tezcatlipoca, Quetzalcoatl, Tlaloc and Chalchiuhtlicue; the last two are not among the four brothers. Codex Vaticanus A starts with the water age instead and gives spans in the thousands of years. The four cartouches around the 4 Movement glyph at the centre of the Sun Stone are conventionally read as jaguar, wind, rain and water, though the monument as a whole remains disputed. The stages below follow the Leyenda de los Soles.

The sequence itself is a modern splice, and there is no guarantee the Mexica told it as one continuous story. The thirteen heavens and the four brothers come from the Historia de los mexicanos; the dismemberment of the earth monster appears only in the Histoyre du Mechique and in no Nahuatl source; the descent into Mictlan and the theft of maize come from the Leyenda de los Soles; the bonfire at Teotihuacan from Sahagún. Different city-states told the story differently, and what follows is the version of the Valley of Mexico. The making of the earth is the roughest join. In the Historia de los mexicanos the earth is made straight out of the body of Cipactli, the monster in the water; in the Histoyre du Mechique the body torn in two is Tlaltecuhtli's. Those are two accounts, later retellings tend to merge them into a single earth monster, and running one into the other, as below, is an editorial choice. Tezcatlipoca's missing foot is a fixture of the imagery, the stump drawn as a smoking obsidian mirror or a serpent, but no sixteenth-century text says how he lost it; the familiar story that the earth monster bit it off comes from later retellings and is kept out of the stages. Two further cautions. Ometeotl as a single supreme dual god is largely a twentieth-century construction, associated above all with Miguel León-Portilla; the name is almost absent from the sixteenth-century sources, which give a divine pair and the place-name Omeyocan, and scholars including Richard Haly have challenged the reading of a single Aztec high god. And Tlaltecuhtli means Earth Lord and is grammatically masculine, though the sculpture of her is overwhelmingly female; the stages below follow the gender used in the Histoyre du Mechique.

title.zh: 第十三重天上的一对神
title.en: The pair in the thirteenth heaven

text.zh: 没有一份文献交代世界之前是什么状态。它们一开口，奥梅特库特利与奥梅西瓦特尔（「二之主」与「二之夫人」，又名托纳卡特库特利与托纳卡西瓦特尔）就已经在奥梅约坎——十三重天里最上的那一重——待着了。《墨西哥人绘画史》只写他们「一直就在那里」，没说来处。婴儿的魂据说是从那一重天上放下来的。这对神自己不动手造东西，动手的是他们生的四个儿子。
text.en: None of the sources says what the state of things was before the world. They begin with Ometecuhtli and Omecihuatl, Lord Two and Lady Two, also called Tonacatecuhtli and Tonacacihuatl, already in place in Omeyocan, the topmost of the thirteen heavens. The Historia de los mexicanos says only that they had always been there, and offers no origin for them. The souls of infants were held to be sent down from that heaven. The pair make nothing themselves. Whatever gets made is made by their four sons.

text.zh: 双神生下四个儿子，各占一色一方：红色的特斯卡特利波卡（在特拉斯卡拉一带叫卡马斯特利）、黑色的特斯卡特利波卡、克察尔科亚特尔，以及墨西加人奉为主神的维齐洛波奇特利。《墨西哥人绘画史》说这四位出生后六百年什么也没做，才开会决定造世界：先造火，再造半个太阳（光不够亮），然后造出第一对男女奥霍莫科与西帕克托纳尔，男的去种地，女的去纺纱，另给她玉米粒用来占卜；接着把日子编成一「月」二十天的历法，造米克特兰特库特利夫妇去管死人的国。最后他们造出水，水里养着一头鱼样的巨兽西帕克特利。
text.en: The pair had four sons, each with a colour and a quarter of the world: the red Tezcatlipoca, known as Camaxtli around Tlaxcala; the black Tezcatlipoca; Quetzalcoatl; and Huitzilopochtli, whom the Mexica took for their own patron. For six hundred years, the Historia de los mexicanos says, the four did nothing whatever. Then they met and got to work. First fire. Then half a sun, which did not give light enough. Then the first man and woman, Oxomoco and Cipactonal: he was set to till the ground, she to spin, and she was given kernels of maize for divining. Then the count of days, twenty to a month. Then Mictlantecuhtli and his wife, to keep the dead. Last they made the water, and in the water a monstrous fish, Cipactli.

text.zh: 大地是从一具活物身上割下来的。据《墨西哥志》，特拉尔特库特利浮在水面，四肢关节处处生着咬人的嘴。特斯卡特利波卡与克察尔科亚特尔化作两条巨蛇缠住她，一条抓右手左脚，一条抓左手右脚，硬把她扯成两截：一截留下当大地，一截举上去做天。别的神看不过去，就定下人所需的一切都从她身上出：头发出树木与花，皮肤出细草和小花，眼睛出泉与小洞，嘴出江河与大穴，鼻子出山谷，肩膀出山岭。她夜里哭着要吃人心，不喂血就不肯结果子。
text.en: The ground was cut out of a living body. In the Histoyre du Mechique, Tlaltecuhtli floats on the water with biting mouths at every joint. Tezcatlipoca and Quetzalcoatl turn themselves into two enormous serpents and take hold of her, one by the right hand and left foot, the other by the left hand and right foot, and pull until she comes apart. One half is left lying as the earth; the other is carried up to be the sky. The rest of the gods, offended at the treatment, ordained that everything people need should come out of her body: trees and flowers from her hair, fine grasses from her skin, springs and small caves from her eyes, rivers and great caverns from her mouth, valleys from her nose, mountains from her shoulders. At night she cries for human hearts, and will not bear fruit unless she is fed blood.

text.zh: 太阳换了四轮，每一轮都以毁灭收场。第一纪「四虎」，676年，那一代人是巨人，被美洲豹啃光。第二纪「四风」，364年，大风把人卷走，剩下的挂在树上变成了猴子。第三纪「四雨」，312年，天上落火，人变成火鸡。第四纪「四水」，676年，天塌水涨，人变成鱼。洪水前特斯卡特利波卡关照塔塔和内内夫妇掏空一株落羽杉钻进去，一人只准带一穗玉米。水退后两人出来，捞了鱼，钻木取火烤着吃，烟直冲上天。星裙女神西特拉利尼奎质问是谁在熏天，特斯卡特利波卡下来割了两人的头，把头安在屁股上，他们成了狗。
text.en: The sun was replaced four times, and each time the world went with it. The first age, 4 Jaguar, ran 676 years; its people were giants, and jaguars ate them. The second, 4 Wind, ran 364 years and ended in a hurricane that carried people off; those left hanging in the trees became monkeys. The third, 4 Rain, ran 312 years, and fire fell out of the sky, and the people became turkeys. The fourth, 4 Water, ran 676 years: the sky came down, the water rose, and everyone became a fish. Before that flood Tezcatlipoca told a married couple, Tata and Nene, to hollow out a cypress and get inside with one ear of maize each and eat nothing else. When the water went down they climbed out, caught a fish, drilled fire and roasted it, and the smoke went straight up. The goddess Citlalinicue, She of the Starry Skirt, demanded to know who was smoking up the sky. Tezcatlipoca came down, cut off their heads, set the heads on their backsides, and left them as dogs.

title.en: Two trees shove the sky back up

text.zh: 洪水那一纪结束时，天整个砸在地上。《墨西哥人绘画史》说，特斯卡特利波卡与克察尔科亚特尔先造了四个人来帮着抬，自己变成两株大树——一株叫特斯卡夸维特（镜树），一株叫克察尔维绍特（绿羽柳）——用树身把天连同星辰重新撑上去。托纳卡特库特利为此立他们做天与星的主人。两位神顺着刚撑起来的天走了一趟，走出来的那道路，就是夜里横过天顶的那条白亮星带。
text.en: When the flood age ended, the whole sky came down on the earth. In the Historia de los mexicanos, Tezcatlipoca and Quetzalcoatl first made four men to help with the lifting, then turned themselves into two great trees, Tezcacuahuitl, the mirror tree, and Quetzalhuexotl, the quetzal willow, and pushed the sky back up with its stars still on it. Tonacatecuhtli made them lords of the sky and the stars for the work. The two of them then walked the length of the sky they had raised, and the road they left behind is the pale band that crosses it at night.

title.zh: 从米克特兰背出来的碎骨
title.en: The broken bones carried out of Mictlan

text.zh: 天地都在了，没人住。克察尔科亚特尔下到死界米克特兰，向米克特兰特库特利讨前几纪人类的骨头。死主开条件：吹响我的海螺，抱着骨头绕我的座位走四圈。那只海螺是实心的，没孔；克察尔科亚特尔叫虫子把它钻通，叫蜂子钻进去，海螺响了。死主转脸反悔，命手下在路上挖坑；鹌鹑一惊，克察尔科亚特尔失足摔进坑里，骨头撒了一地，被鹌鹑啃得零零碎碎。他爬起来把碎骨包好背回塔莫安昌，女神基拉斯特利（即西瓦科亚特尔）在石磨上把骨头磨成粉，盛进玉钵。他刺破自己的阳具往粉里滴血，别的神也一个个放血苦修——人类因此叫「马塞瓦尔廷」，意思是「用苦修换来的那些人」。人有了还没得吃：他变成一只黑蚂蚁，跟着红蚂蚁钻进粮山托纳卡特佩特尔，叼出一粒玉米，众神嚼碎了抹在婴儿嘴上。后来纳纳瓦特尔用雷把粮山劈开，白、黑、黄、红四色玉米连同豆子和苋籽，被雨神特拉洛克们抢走。
text.en: Sky and earth were in place and nobody lived on them. Quetzalcoatl went down to Mictlan and asked Mictlantecuhtli for the bones of the people of the earlier ages. The death lord set a condition: blow my conch trumpet, and carry the bones four times around my seat. The conch was solid, with no holes bored in it, so Quetzalcoatl had worms drill it through and bees crawl inside until it sounded. Mictlantecuhtli then changed his mind and had a pit dug across the road out. Quail startled Quetzalcoatl, he fell in, and the bones scattered and broke, and the quail chewed on them. He got up, bundled what was left, and carried it to Tamoanchan, where the goddess Quilaztli, who is Cihuacoatl, ground the bones on a stone metate into a bowl of jade. Quetzalcoatl drew blood from his own member onto the meal, and the other gods bled and did penance with him. That is why people are macehualtin, the ones obtained by penance. They still had nothing to eat, so he turned himself into a black ant, followed a red ant into Tonacatepetl, the mountain of sustenance, and brought out a single grain of maize, which the gods chewed and put on the lips of the infants. Nanahuatl afterwards split the mountain open with lightning, and the Tlaloque made off with the white, black, yellow and red maize, and the beans and amaranth with it.

text.zh: 天还是黑的。诸神在特奥蒂瓦坎聚了四天，架起一座大火堆，问谁肯跳进去当太阳。有钱的特克西斯特卡特尔当场应了；再问第二个，没人接话，众神就点了满身脓疮的小神纳纳瓦特辛。两人各修行四天：特克西斯特卡特尔拿绿咬鹃羽当松枝、金球当草球、玉刺当龙舌兰刺、红珊瑚代替血染的刺，烧的是最好的柯巴香；纳纳瓦特辛只有三束绿芦苇、几个干草球、真的沾着自己血的龙舌兰刺，当香烧的是从自己疮上抠下来的痂。火烧了四天四夜。特克西斯特卡特尔冲到火边四次，四次被热气逼回去。纳纳瓦特辛闭上眼一头扎进去，烧得噼啪作响；特克西斯特卡特尔这才跟着跳。一只鹰扑进火里，羽毛烧焦；一只美洲豹随后跳过，燎出一身斑点。
text.en: It was still dark. The gods gathered at Teotihuacan for four days, built a great fire, and asked which of them would jump in and become the sun. Rich Tecciztecatl volunteered on the spot. When they asked for a second, nobody spoke, so they named Nanahuatzin, the small god covered in running sores. Each did four days of penance. Tecciztecatl brought quetzal plumes in place of fir branches, balls of gold in place of grass, jade in place of maguey spines, red coral in place of spines bloodied by the piercing, and the best copal to burn. Nanahuatzin had three bundles of green reeds, balls of dry grass, maguey spines actually stained with his own blood, and for incense the scabs he picked off his sores. The fire burned four days and nights. Tecciztecatl ran at it four times, and four times the heat turned him back. Nanahuatzin shut his eyes and threw himself in, and burned, crackling. Only then did Tecciztecatl follow. An eagle went in after them and came out with its feathers scorched, and a jaguar after the eagle, singed into spots.

text.zh: 太阳从东边升起来，月亮跟着升起来，一样亮。诸神说这不行，抓起一只兔子照特克西斯特卡特尔脸上摔过去，月亮暗了一层，兔子的影子至今留在上面。可太阳只在天边摇晃，不肯走，开口要血、要众神称臣。埃埃卡特尔（风神形态的克察尔科亚特尔）便一个个剖开诸神的胸膛。修洛特尔不肯死，哭到眼珠掉出来，先变成双穗玉米，再变成双心龙舌兰，最后变成水里的蝾螈阿霍洛特尔，还是被捉住杀了。众神死尽，太阳依然不动，埃埃卡特尔鼓足力气吹，它才上了路，月亮跟在后头。这一纪叫「四运动」（纳维·奥林）。它会以地震收场：大地摇散，星魔齐齐米梅从天上落下来吃人。让太阳继续走，是墨西加人认下的一笔债，人祭与放血都算在这笔账上。
text.en: The sun came up in the east, and then the moon came up just as bright. That would not do, so one of the gods snatched up a rabbit and struck Tecciztecatl in the face with it; the moon has been the dimmer of the two ever since, and the rabbit is still on it. But the sun only swayed at the horizon and would not travel, and it demanded blood and the submission of the gods. So Ehecatl, Quetzalcoatl in his wind form, opened their chests one by one. Xolotl would not die: he wept until his eyes fell out of their sockets, fled first as a double-eared maize plant, then as a split maguey, then as a salamander in the water, the axolotl, and was caught and killed all the same. With every god dead the sun still would not stir, and Ehecatl had to blow with all his force before it took the road, the moon coming along behind. This age is 4 Movement, Nahui Ollin. It ends in earthquakes: the ground tears itself apart and the tzitzimime, the star demons, come down to eat what is left. Keeping the sun in motion was a debt the Mexica acknowledged, and they reckoned human sacrifice and their own bloodletting against it.
